data.title: Edison International targets 5% to 7% EPS growth through 2030 while reaffirming 2028 outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Edison International (EIX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Pedro Pizarro, President and CEO, announced "Edison International's 2025 core earnings per share of $6.55 was above our guidance range, that extends our 2-decade track record of meeting or exceeding annual EPS guidance." He emphasized the successful delivery of the long-term core EPS growth target established in 2021 and highlighted disciplined execution, cost management, and operational performance.
  • Pizarro underscored commitments to "customers, communities and investors," regulatory visibility, and "confidence in our multiyear plan." He noted a 2.3% rate decrease for residential customers and a 5.3% decrease for small- and medium-sized business customers, along with SCE's installation of more than 7,000 miles of covered conductor in high fire risk areas.
  • Pizarro detailed enhancements to the wildfire recovery compensation program, including "stronger support for displaced renters and increasing coverage for legal expenses." He addressed the Eaton fire, stating SCE "is not aware of evidence pointing to another possible source of ignition" and that it is likely SCE equipment could have been associated with the ignition. SCE is unable to reasonably estimate a range of potential losses at this time.
  • Pizarro noted regulatory clarity for 2026 after the completion of major proceedings, and introduced guidance for 2026 and 2027, reaffirmed the 2028 outlook, and extended the EPS growth target through 2030. He stated, "With an attractive dividend yield of approximately 5%, and a long-term core EPS growth target of 5% to 7%, EIX shares offer a compelling case for total shareholder returns of 10% to 12%."
  • Maria Rigatti, Executive VP & CFO, stated "For the fourth quarter, EIX reported core EPS of $1.86. Full year 2025 core EPS of $6.55 exceeded the high end of our EPS guidance range." Rigatti highlighted cost impacts from preferred stock actions and a $0.46 true-up related to the Woolsey cost recovery.

Outlook

  • Rigatti introduced core EPS guidance for 2026 as $5.90 to $6.20, and for 2027 as $6.25 to $6.65. She explained "2026 core EPS represents growth of about 3.5% at the midpoint compared to the $5.84 baseline," with muted growth driven by fewer regulatory decisions, asset mix differences, and financing/tax variances, which are expected to be non-recurring. Rigatti added, "we expect EPS growth in 2027 to be at the high end of our 5% to 7% range."
  • The company reaffirmed its 2028 guidance and extended the 5% to 7% EPS growth target through 2030. Rigatti stated, "we project no equity needs for the next five years through 2030."

Financial Results

  • EIX reported Q4 2025 core EPS of $1.86 and full year core EPS of $6.55. Rigatti noted that the results included $0.06 of costs from preferred stock transactions and a $0.46 true-up from the Woolsey cost recovery.
  • SCE plans $7 billion in capital expenditures for 2026. The updated capital plan for 2026–2030 is $38 billion to $41 billion, with projected rate base growth of approximately 7% from 2025 to 2030. The plan now includes nearly $1.5 billion of capital expenditures from the upcoming AMI 2.0 application.
  • SCE filed its Woolsey Securitization application to securitize about $2 billion in settlement costs, with proceeds expected to offset normal debt issuances if approved.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: Asked about Eaton fire losses and timing for estimating total liabilities. Pizarro responded that "we really don't have an estimate for that yet because it really depends on the pace of this," noting only 2,300 claims submitted so far out of a much larger pool. Rigatti clarified the $1 billion recorded includes both WRCP payments and subrogation settlements.
  • Campanella also asked about 5% to 7% growth at the high end in 2027 and considerations for 2028 and 2029. Rigatti explained, "as you get out to '29 and '30, again, right back down to rate base growth. I mean, that is really the driver here."
  • Carly Davenport, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about timing for AMI 2.0 approval and impact on the capital plan. Rigatti stated the filing is expected in the next few months, with a decision hoped for in 18 months, and clarified the capital allocation through 2033. Davenport also asked about SB 254 process progress, with Pizarro describing robust stakeholder engagement and emphasizing the need for a predictable framework for capital access.
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked if 8% rate base growth from 2028–2030 indicates faster earnings trajectory. Rigatti responded, "I would focus on that now. I think we're not seeing anything other than rate base growth as we move out in time."
  • Ryan Levine, Citi: Sought rationale for recent tweaks to the wildfire compensation program. Pizarro detailed adjustments to support tenants and increase attorney fee coverage, applied retroactively, emphasizing a stable, understandable program.
  • Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan: Asked about the LA District Attorney's investigation regarding criminal violations in the Eaton fire and grounding of the out-of-service transmission tower. Pizarro confirmed the line was grounded at both ends and discussed changes to protocols, adding more grounding points for idle lines.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a neutral to slightly cautious tone, focusing on wildfire liabilities, regulatory clarity, and capital planning. Questions probed for specificity on loss estimates, capital deployment, and regulatory outcomes.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone, using phrases like "we are confident" and "we have the right strategy, the right plan and the right team in place." In the Q&A, responses were thorough and transparent, with Rigatti and Pizarro acknowledging uncertainty where appropriate and providing clarification on recurring questions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from probing legislative outcomes and regulatory risk to seeking more clarity on execution and financial impact of wildfire liabilities. Management’s tone remained confident, with increased emphasis on regulatory stability and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was previously set for 2025 and reaffirmed for 2028, but in this quarter, EIX extended its 5% to 7% EPS growth target through 2030 and introduced guidance for 2026 and 2027.
  • The previous quarter focused on significant legislative progress (SB 254), GRC approval, and settlement agreements. This quarter, the focus shifted to execution of these frameworks, capital plan updates, and enhancements to wildfire compensation programs.
  • Analysts in both quarters raised concerns about wildfire liabilities and regulatory clarity, but the current quarter included more questions on program specifics, loss estimation, and capital timing.
  • Management’s confidence remained strong, but the current quarter emphasized execution on capital plans, affirmed no equity needs through 2030, and discussed programmatic adjustments in response to community feedback.

Risks and Concerns

  • Uncertainty remains regarding total losses from the Eaton fire, with management unable to estimate the low end of potential liabilities due to the ongoing pace of claim submissions.
  • The LA District Attorney’s investigation into the Eaton fire adds legal uncertainty, though management expressed confidence in SCE’s conduct.
  • Regulatory and legislative developments, especially around SB 254 and the CEA report, continue to pose potential risks but are being actively monitored by management.
  • Management pointed to ongoing engagement with policymakers to reinforce regulatory durability.

Final Takeaway

Edison International closed 2025 having surpassed its core EPS guidance and delivered on its long-term growth commitments, supported by disciplined execution, regulatory clarity, and robust capital planning. The company reaffirmed and extended its 5% to 7% EPS growth target through 2030, introduced guidance for 2026 and 2027, and outlined a multiyear capital plan with no equity needs anticipated through 2030. While risks from wildfire liabilities and ongoing investigations remain, management emphasized its commitment to operational excellence, customer affordability, and maintaining a stable regulatory framework to drive shareholder value and support California’s clean energy objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GameStop's Ryan Cohen takes on the 'hollow men' of corporate boardrooms
data.description:
GameStop (GME) CEO Ryan Cohen took to social media site X on Wednesday to make the case that American capitalism is rotting from the head down. The timing is intriguing, with GameStop (GME) reportedly on the hunt for an acquisition that could reset investor sentiment on the stock.

Cohen warned that the idea of an owner-operator or risk-taker has been replaced with a new, parasitic class of corporate bureaucrat that he called the risk-free insider and the hollow men of the boardroom. 

Key snippets of the missive are below.

"They are masters of PowerPoint. They wear the right suits. They say the right buzzwords about governance and ESG. But they are mercenaries fighting a war with someone else’s ammunition. In a functioning economy, authority is tied to liability. If you make a bad decision, you lose your own money. That fear of loss is the only thing that keeps a business honest. It forces you to cut waste, obsess over the customer, and stay late to fix what is broken. Today, we have severed that link."

"If the stock goes up, the Insider collects a massive performance bonus. If the stock crashes due to their own incompetence, they are fired with a 'Golden Parachute' worth tens of millions. They are gambling with the house’s money, and they never leave the table poorer than they arrived."

"They use shareholder capital to buy an insurance policy for their own careers. If the plan fails, they can blame the consultants. They delegate the work because they are terrified of the responsibility. They would rather preside over a slow, comfortable decline than risk a bold mistake."

"The time for polite governance is over. If we want to save the American economy from mediocrity, we must demand a return to the "Owner’s Mentality." We need leaders who treat shareholder capital with the same reverence they treat their own savings. The era of the Risk-Free Insider must end."

data.title: Cheesecake Factory signals up to 26 new units in 2026 while raising dividend and share repurchase authorization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • David Overton, Founder, Chairman & CEO, reported, "We closed out the year with a solid fourth quarter, delivering stable top line performance and profitability. While the restaurant industry continued to face a more challenging operating environment, including weather-related impacts, our business remained steady with revenue for the quarter finishing within our expected range." He highlighted record annual revenue and adjusted diluted earnings per share, and noted "the most new restaurant openings in a single year." Overton also announced, "We announced an increase to our share repurchase authorization and raised our quarterly dividend for the first quarter."
  • David Gordon, President, stated that recent menu additions have led to "year-over-year growth in appetizer attachment rates and improved entree ordering patterns." Gordon emphasized ongoing menu innovation and the launch of a dedicated rewards app in the coming months for enhanced guest engagement.
  • Matthew Clark, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "Total revenues were $961.6 million, inclusive of $17.3 million of gift card breakage revenue." Clark added, "Adjusted diluted earnings per share was $1, both finishing toward the higher end of our expectations. And we returned $24 million to our shareholders in the form of dividends and stock repurchases."

Outlook

  • Clark outlined, "For Q1, we anticipate total revenues to be between $955 million and $970 million. This includes the estimated impact of inclement weather experienced so far in the quarter and 4 restaurant closures that occurred toward the end of January." He further projected, "We anticipate total revenues for fiscal 2026 to be approximately $3.9 billion at the midpoint of our sensitivity modeling."
  • The company expects to open as many as 26 new restaurants in 2026, with the majority of openings planned for the second half of the year. Approximately $210 million in cash CapEx is anticipated to support development and maintenance.
  • Clark stated, "We are estimating preopening expenses to be approximately $35 million to $36 million" for 2026, and "full year net income margin to be approximately 5% at the sales estimate provided."

Financial Results

  • Clark reported, "Fourth quarter total sales at The Cheesecake Factory restaurants were $681.4 million, up 2% from the prior year." Excluding gift card breakage, sales were $664.2 million. Comparable sales at The Cheesecake Factory declined 2.2% versus the prior year.
  • North Italia's sales reached $88.2 million for the quarter, up 8% year-over-year, with comparable sales down 4%. Flower Child delivered sales of $45.5 million, up 19%, and comparable sales up 4%. FRC sales totaled $99.4 million, up 17%, and external bakery sales were $17.2 million.
  • Adjusted net income margin was 5.1% and adjusted diluted earnings per share was $1 for the quarter. The company ended the quarter with $582.2 million in available liquidity and $644 million in debt.

Q&A

  • Andrew Barish, Jefferies: Asked about FRC management structure and changes. Clark responded, "All of the lines of business are meeting or exceeding our expectations...we'll continue to look at opportunities to add benefits via scale or expertise in operations."
  • Sara Senatore, BofA: Inquired about margin-supported value investments and menu pricing. Gordon confirmed, "We're seeing strong attachment rates because they do provide great Cheesecake Factory value...we're going to lead into that value wherever we can."
  • Brian Harbour, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarification on weather impact for Q1. Clark answered, "The weather to date through Q1, we believe, is about a 1% net impact -- negative net impact on the entire quarter."
  • Andrew North, Baird: Asked about consumer spending trends and outlook. Clark said, "I do think coming out into the first quarter here that our performance is notably better...our performance, if you interpolate the guidance, is more like what we saw in Q2 of last year."
  • James Salera, Stephens: Probed on traffic, mix impact, and demand drivers for 2026. Clark replied, "Q4 pricing was about 3.5% to 4% and mix was a negative 1.8%...the negative mix will continue, but at a lesser rate as we progress through the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin sustainability, menu value, traffic and mix outlook, and potential impacts from weather and consumer trends, with a tone that was neutral to slightly cautious on the macro environment.
  • Management maintained a confident, steady tone, emphasizing operational execution and resilience, especially in prepared remarks. Clark used phrases such as "we remain confident" and described performance as "solid," while also acknowledging challenges such as weather and sales softness. During Q&A, responses remained detailed and constructive, with some cautious language around consumer trends and mix headwinds.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was similarly confident but more focused on addressing recent industry challenges and weather impacts. Analyst tone remained consistent, with continued attention to sales trends and margin dynamics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 revenue is more specific at $3.9 billion midpoint, compared to a 4% to 5% growth range previously projected for 2026.
  • The company reported higher adjusted diluted earnings per share and record adjusted EBITDA for the year. Unit growth accelerated, with 25 new restaurant openings in 2025 versus earlier projections.
  • Management increased share repurchase authorization and the quarterly dividend, signaling greater confidence in cash flow and capital allocation.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin, mix, traffic, and the impact of new menu items, with weather and macro factors more prominent in the current quarter.
  • Management's confidence in operational execution and menu innovation remained strong, while more cautious commentary emerged regarding near-term consumer and weather headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited challenging industry conditions, including weather-related impacts and a decelerating sales environment in Q4.
  • Clark highlighted, "We anticipate total inflation across our commodity basket, labor and other operating expenses to be in the low to mid-single-digit range."
  • Weather disruptions and four recent restaurant closures were factored into Q1 guidance. Analysts raised questions about consumer demand volatility, mix headwinds from menu innovation, and regional performance variability.
  • Management emphasized a focus on disciplined operational execution and ongoing menu innovation to mitigate risks.

Final Takeaway

The Cheesecake Factory closed 2025 with record annual revenue and earnings, supported by strong operational execution, disciplined capital allocation, and accelerated new unit development. Management signaled confidence in the business by raising its dividend and share repurchase authorization, projecting revenues of approximately $3.9 billion in 2026, and planning to open as many as 26 restaurants. While acknowledging macro and weather-related challenges, the company remains focused on menu innovation, efficient operations, and consistent guest experience to drive future growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EBay outlines 10–12% GMV growth target for Q1 2026 while advancing C2C strategy through Depop acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: eBay Inc. (EBAY) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jamie Iannone shared that "we finished 2025 with incredible momentum as we delivered Q4 results that meaningfully exceeded our expectations." He highlighted that gross merchandise volume (GMV) grew by nearly 6% to approximately $80 billion globally, with U.S. GMV up nearly 10% for the year. Revenue increased by nearly 7% to $11.1 billion, outpacing GMV growth, notably driven by advertising, which reached approximately $2 billion in annual revenue. Iannone emphasized the strategic expansion in focus categories, consumer-to-consumer (C2C), and recommerce, which together comprised 2/3 of eBay's business and generated roughly $50 billion in unique GMV with approximately 10% growth. Innovations such as AI-powered magical listings and direct collaborations with brands were spotlighted as key drivers.
  • Iannone announced a definitive agreement to acquire Depop for approximately $1.2 billion in cash, describing it as a "natural strategic and cultural fit" that strengthens eBay's C2C value proposition and expands its presence in the circular fashion segment.
  • CFO Peggy Alford stated, "GMV grew over 8% to $21.2 billion. Revenue grew over 13% to $2.96 billion. Our non-GAAP operating income grew over 11% year-over-year to $775 million. Non-GAAP earnings per share grew nearly 13% year-over-year to $1.41 and we returned $756 million to shareholders through repurchases and cash dividends."

Outlook

  • Alford provided Q1 2026 guidance, expecting GMV between $21.5 billion and $21.9 billion, representing total FX-neutral growth between 10% and 12% year-over-year. Revenue is forecasted between $3 billion and $3.05 billion, implying FX-neutral growth of 13% to 15%. Non-GAAP operating income growth is projected between 11% and 16%, with non-GAAP earnings per share guidance between $1.53 and $1.59, representing 12% to 16% year-over-year growth. For the full year 2026, eBay is planning for GMV growth similar to 2025 and revenue growth in line to slightly ahead of GMV.
  • Alford explained, "We expect non-GAAP operating income growth between 8% and 10% year-over-year in 2026," and noted that the Depop acquisition is expected to contribute 1 to 2 percentage points to total FX-neutral GMV growth year-over-year, assuming the deal closes as expected in Q2.

Financial Results

  • GMV for Q4 grew over 8% to $21.2 billion. Revenue increased over 13% to $2.96 billion. Non-GAAP operating income grew over 11% year-over-year to $775 million. Non-GAAP earnings per share was $1.41, up nearly 13% year-over-year. Free cash flow was $478 million in Q4. eBay ended the year with $4.8 billion in cash and fixed income investments and $6.7 billion in gross debt. The company repurchased $625 million of shares in Q4 and paid a quarterly dividend of $0.29 per share.
  • Focus category GMV grew over 16% in Q4, with all categories contributing positively, led by collectibles, P&A, luxury, refurbished apparel, and sneakers. U.S. GMV grew nearly 19%, while international GMV declined nearly 1%. Active buyers totaled roughly 135 million in Q4, with enthusiast buyers stable at 16 million and spend per enthusiast buyer over $3,300.

Q&A

  • Nikhil Devnani, Bernstein: Asked about the drivers of sustained U.S. acceleration and new growth vectors. Iannone responded that "years of investment that we've made are paying off," highlighting the strength across focus categories, C2C, and recommerce and noting "we see the broad-based nature of our growth and that momentum really carrying through to 2026."
  • Colin Sebastian, Baird: Inquired about international competition and AI rollout. Iannone noted, "the focus category stuff that we've rolled out internationally has been performing well," while C2C initiatives and eBay Live expansion are supporting growth, but macro conditions in Europe remain challenging. On agentic AI, he underscored eBay's differentiated inventory and trust infrastructure.
  • Ross Sandler, Barclays: Asked about the cadence of growth and Depop integration. Alford mentioned "a significant amount of the acceleration that we saw from Q4 to Q1 was related to just the bullion and collectible uptick" but emphasized confidence in the durability of core growth. Iannone described Depop as "a great strategic fit for eBay" given its younger demographic and synergy with the C2C and fashion segments.
  • Nathaniel Feather, Morgan Stanley: Sought details on Depop synergies and GMV drivers. Iannone cited parallels to eBay's collectibles integrations and plans to leverage back-end services for Depop. Alford clarified that the strength in GMV is broad-based, not solely attributable to bullion and coins.
  • Shweta Khajuria, Wolfe Research: Asked about EPS growth drivers and agentic commerce partnerships. Alford reiterated the focus on organic investment and capital returns. Iannone confirmed participation in the OpenAI Ads Pilot Program and emphasized eBay's unique position in agentic commerce.
  • Tom Champion, Piper Sandler: Requested an update on eBay Live and Facebook partnership. Iannone said, "it's really a natural extension of our marketplace," and described eBay Live as contributing to double-digit growth in focus categories, attracting new buyers and engaging sellers. On Facebook, he noted expansion of eBay inventory into Facebook Search.
  • Michael Morton, MoffettNathanson: Asked about trading card sustainability and agentic traffic. Iannone stated, "we continue to see a long runway for secular growth in trading cards," and noted high intent and conversion from agentic AI traffic, though overall volumes are currently small.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on the durability of recent growth, sustainability of new categories, and the impact of acquisitions. The tone was largely positive, but with probing on specifics, such as agentic commerce, EPS growth drivers, and integration of new growth vectors.
  • Management expressed high confidence throughout, with Iannone stating, "the underlying health of the business is the strongest it's been since I've joined the company 6 years ago." Alford consistently described strong results and discipline in capital returns. Tone remained confident and measured, similar to the previous quarter, but with increased emphasis on the breadth and momentum of growth.
  • Compared to Q3, management's tone is more optimistic, citing accelerated growth and new strategic directions, while analysts maintained a neutral-to-positive, inquisitive stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution about "lapping dynamics" in Q3 to a more confident outlook for consistent GMV and revenue growth in 2026. Management highlighted broader momentum in focus categories and C2C, while international macro pressures and U.S. policy impacts continued to be noted.
  • The Q4 call introduced a major strategic shift with the pending acquisition of Depop, extending the C2C and fashion footprint and aiming for synergy with eBay's core marketplace. Q3 emphasized the Tise acquisition and core category innovation.
  • GMV and revenue growth rates accelerated in Q4 vs. Q3, and management showed increased confidence in operational execution, particularly in the U.S. market.
  • Analyst focus expanded from margins and cost structures in Q3 to sustainability of category growth, integration of acquisitions, and technology-driven initiatives in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that international GMV declined nearly 1% due to macroeconomic conditions in Europe and trade policy changes impacting U.S. imports.
  • Alford flagged that "losses were primarily attributable to our recently launched shipping programs, which significantly improved the seller and buyer experience," though they are expected to decline over time.
  • Accounting policy changes, including expensing all product development costs and switching revenue recognition for the U.K. managed shipping program, are expected to modestly pressure take rate and operating income in 2026.
  • The pending Depop acquisition is projected to be a "low single-digit headwind" to 2026 operating income and non-GAAP EPS growth, with long-term accretion expected by 2028.

Final Takeaway

eBay's Q4 2025 results underscored broad-based momentum in focus categories, C2C, and recommerce, with significant top- and bottom-line growth, particularly in the U.S. The pending Depop acquisition marks a major strategic move to accelerate the C2C and fashion marketplace, while disciplined capital allocation and continued AI-driven innovations position the company for durable growth in 2026. Management expressed strong confidence in the health of the business and the ability to build on this foundation with emerging growth vectors and technology advancements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Booking Holdings targets 100 basis points faster revenue growth in 2026 with $700M reinvestment strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Glenn Fogel, President, CEO & Director, opened by stating, "2025 was another year of strong execution for us. Despite volatility in the broader global markets, the underlying fundamentals of our business are solid. Travel demand remains resilient, and we continue to benefit from our existing global platform." Fogel highlighted the deployment of AI-powered capabilities, progress on strategic priorities, and a focus on the Connected Trip vision, Asia and U.S. growth strategies, and AI advancements.
  • Fogel reported, "Our fourth quarter room nights reached 285 million, a 9% year-over-year increase. This exceeded the high end of our expectations, driven by healthy demand across all of our major regions. The better-than-expected room night growth helped drive fourth quarter gross bookings and revenue, both up 16%. Adjusted EBITDA reached $2.2 billion, up 19% from the prior year quarter. Finally, adjusted earnings per share grew 17% year-over-year."
  • Fogel emphasized the Transformation Program launched in November 2024, which "has already enabled approximately $550 million in annual run rate savings, which was the high end of our prior guidance."
  • The Genius loyalty program was noted as a key driver, with Level 2 and 3 Genius travelers representing "over 30% of our active base and accounted for a high 50% share of room nights, up from the previous year."
  • Fogel described Asia as "one of the most attractive growth opportunities," citing low double-digit room night growth in the region for 2025.
  • Fogel stated, "In 2025, we focused on rolling out agentic capabilities across our brands that enhance the full traveler journey, helping customers discover and plan trips through natural language search, make more informed booking decisions with smart filters and summaries and get better, faster support before and during their trip through interactive AI agents."
  • Ewout Steenbergen, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "We finished 2025 with a strong fourth quarter...Our room nights in the fourth quarter grew 9%, exceeding the high end of our guidance range by 3 percentage points as we saw benefits from our continued investments into Asia and the U.S. and the booking window was more expanded than expected."
  • Steenbergen also announced, "Our Board of Directors approved a 9.4% increase to our quarterly cash dividend per share to $10.50 per share. This reflects our confidence in our long-term earnings trajectory... We're also pleased to announce that our Board of Directors has approved a 25-for-1 stock split. This split will take effect on April 2, and we will begin trading on a post-split basis starting April 6."

Outlook

  • Steenbergen indicated, "Assuming 2026 travel industry growth is in line with recent years, we are targeting full year constant currency top line growth of approximately 100 basis points ahead of our long-term growth algorithm while keeping bottom line performance firmly in line with that framework." The company aims to deliver constant currency gross bookings and revenue growth "that is about 100 basis points faster than our long-term growth ambition of 8% while expanding adjusted EBITDA margins by approximately 50 basis points."
  • For the first quarter of 2026, room night growth is expected to be "between 5% and 7%." First quarter gross bookings and revenue growth are both expected to be "between 14% and 16%." Adjusted EBITDA growth for Q1 is guided to be "between 10% and 14%."
  • The company plans to reinvest about $700 million above baseline in 2026 into areas including GenAI, Connected Trip, Asia and U.S. growth, advertising, OpenTable's international expansion, and fintech and loyalty offerings.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter gross bookings increased 16% year-over-year, with revenue also up 16%. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $2.2 billion, up 19% year-over-year. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $48.80 per share, up 17% year-over-year.
  • Full year 2025 gross bookings and revenue grew 12% and 13%, respectively. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was over $9.9 billion, a 20% year-over-year increase. Adjusted EBITDA margin reached 36.9%, up 193 basis points year-over-year.
  • The company generated $9.1 billion in free cash flow for the year and returned $8.2 billion to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends.
  • Merchant gross bookings were $130 billion, representing about 70% of total gross bookings, and airline ticket bookings reached 68 million, up 37% year-over-year. Alternative accommodation room night growth was about 10% for the year.

Q&A

  • Mark Stephen Mahaney, Evercore: Asked about marketing spend deleverage in Q4 and future approach. Fogel responded that the company is "tactical" and "strike[s] while the iron is hot," emphasizing long-term value over short-term deleverage. Steenbergen added, "We saw also continued expansion in our social media investments. Some great opportunities there leaning in with some other social media platforms...overall, 13% growth in terms of investments in social media marketing."
  • Lee Horowitz, Deutsche Bank: Asked about economic uplift from agentic capabilities and rollout cadence. Fogel declined to specify details but said, "We're keen to build out all sorts of new ways to do agentic -- different parts of the systems where agentic capabilities are being brought in." Steenbergen noted, "More engagement from our traveler customers, faster search, better conversion, lower cancellation rates and positive customer satisfaction."
  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about Genius program signals and future investments. Fogel said, "Genius is a wonderful program, but it's not nearly what it can be and will be in the future...we want to increase that loyalty." Steenbergen added that Level 2 and 3 Genius customers now contribute to the "high 50% level" of room nights, indicating higher direct booking rates.
  • Alex Brignall, Rothschild & Co: Requested updates on partnerships with agentic businesses and merchant of record dynamics. Fogel stated, "It's very early. There's not a lot to show right now," and detailed the complexity of being a merchant of record, emphasizing Booking Holdings' competitive strengths in connectivity and payments.
  • Lloyd Walmsley, Mizuho: Asked about growth in paid search channels and investment allocation. Fogel said, "search is good. We're doing good business." Steenbergen detailed that investments are spreading across advertising, social media, loyalty, Connected Trip, generative AI, attractions, and fintech.
  • Trevor Young, Barclays: Questioned alternative accommodation growth and future expectations. Fogel stated, "the alternative accommodation area is a very important area for us in the future...we got more to be done." Steenbergen reported "healthy growth of alternative accommodation supply...now at 8.6 million listings."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on long-term value and competitive positioning, raising questions about marketing spend, AI-driven solutions, loyalty programs, search channel dynamics, and alternative accommodations. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, probing for detail rather than expressing concern.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout both prepared remarks and Q&A, with frequent reiterations of discipline, strategic investment, and excitement about future growth. Fogel used positive language including "excited," "proud," and "confident," while Steenbergen provided detailed, upbeat financial context. Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remained optimistic, with a stronger emphasis on reinvestment and innovation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more explicit, targeting constant currency revenue growth 100 basis points above the long-term algorithm and expanded EBITDA margins, compared to the prior quarter's focus on exceeding long-term expectations.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward larger reinvestments ($700 million above baseline) and a significant increase in Transformation Program savings guidance for 2026.
  • Analysts continued to probe on marketing allocation, AI strategies, and the Genius program, but with more emphasis on the economic impact of AI and reinvestment cadence this quarter.
  • Management's tone remained confident, with increased specificity on planned investments and operational leverage, compared to the previous quarter's broader outlook and focus on current competitive positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the continued need for operational efficiency and cost controls, citing the Transformation Program as a key enabler.
  • Fogel highlighted the complexity of acting as a merchant of record, especially in the context of regulation and global payments, as an ongoing operational challenge.
  • Analysts flagged potential for marketing spend deleverage and the challenge of maintaining alternative accommodation growth, but management asserted these areas remain core to the strategy and are supported by ongoing investment.

Final Takeaway

Booking Holdings delivered strong Q4 and full-year 2025 results, with record growth across room nights, gross bookings, and adjusted EBITDA. Management emphasized disciplined reinvestment, targeting a step-up in both revenue growth and margin expansion for 2026, powered by AI, loyalty, and Connected Trip initiatives. The Board's approval of a dividend increase and a 25-for-1 stock split further signals confidence in sustained shareholder value creation. The company remains focused on operational excellence, innovation, and expanding its leadership in global travel, with a particular emphasis on Asia, fintech, and AI-driven customer engagement.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bausch Health expects 3% revenue growth in 2026 while strengthening balance sheet and expanding in China
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bausch Health Companies Inc. (BHC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Appio highlighted the company's "11th consecutive quarter of growth in both revenue and adjusted EBITDA," with full year results "exceeded our guidance on all key metrics." Appio emphasized the company's "improved capital structure" and continued "operational efficiencies," noting proactive business development initiatives and a strategic acquisition in China.
  • Appio reported, "On December 1, 2025, we acquired Shibo, full-service aesthetics distribution platform in China, strengthening our direct commercial presence in this key market." He described this as a move to enhance Bausch Health's reach in one of the largest global aesthetics markets.
  • The CEO also noted robust performance in products such as Xifaxan and Thermage, stating "Xifaxan revenue grew 11% for the year," and "Thermage revenue grew a robust 19%."
  • Appio addressed the RED-C program, acknowledging, "We are disappointed by the outcome we announced in January that while safe and well tolerated, neither Phase III trial met its primary endpoint. We are currently reviewing the full data set."
  • CFO Jean-Jacques Charhon stated, "Revenue was $2.796 billion, up 9% on a reported basis compared to the same quarter a year ago. Adjusted gross margin was 71.6%, which was 80 basis points lower than the same period a year ago." Charhon also noted, "Adjusted EBITDA was $1.52 billion in the fourth quarter, an increase of 13% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Charhon provided 2026 guidance for Bausch Health, excluding Bausch + Lomb: "We expect revenues to be between $5.25 billion and $5.4 billion. The midpoint of that range would translate into a 3% increase year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $2.875 billion and $2.950 billion, representing a 4% increase year-over-year at the midpoint. Finally, we expect adjusted operating cash flow to be between $1.2 billion and $1.275 billion."
  • Management stated that "from a phasing perspective, we anticipate a stronger growth rate in the first half of 2026, given the temporary nature of some of the benefits we recorded in the second half of 2025."
  • Charhon emphasized, "There is no major change versus what we set out last year, making 2026 an extension of what we accomplished in 2025."

Financial Results

  • Bausch Health, excluding Bausch + Lomb, reported fourth quarter revenue of $1.391 billion, a 9% increase on a reported basis. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $773 million, a 9% increase from the previous year.
  • Adjusted operating cash flow for the fourth quarter was $362 million, down $205 million year-over-year, which Charhon attributed to "the change in timing of our cash interest payments following the refinancing we executed on April 8, 2025."
  • Salix segment revenues were $693 million, an increase of 9% year-over-year, "ahead of expectations" due to higher-than-planned volume from certain state Medicaid customers.
  • International segment revenues were $306 million, up 10% reported, with EMEA and LatAm growing double digit, while Canada contracted 6% due to increased generic competition.
  • Solta Medical segment revenues were $137 million, down 1% reported, with management noting a one-time impact from the distributor transition in China.
  • Charhon outlined improvements to the capital structure: "We repaid our $300 million accounts receivable facility and... completed a $1.7 billion secured debt exchange, which allowed us to push out maturities for four years."

Q&A

  • JP, Evercore ISI, asked about the decision framework for separating Bausch + Lomb after RED-C and debt repayments. Appio responded, "there's no change... we continue to focus on repaying debt and reinvesting in our business... engaging in business development activities, which is one of the things we are accelerating now that we have significantly changed our capital structure with the refinancing."
  • Leszek Sulewski, Truist Securities, asked about the Shibo integration and the impact on Solta. Charhon answered, "Net-net, it's about a $10 million to $15 million hit from an EBITDA perspective in the quarter."
  • Michael Freeman, Raymond James, questioned if 2026 will be a peak year for Xifaxan sales. Charhon explained, "2025 might be the peak year for Xifaxan just because we had some onetime benefits in the year that will not repeat in 2026."
  • Glen Santangelo, Barclays, raised concerns regarding EBITDA impact in 2027 from IRA pricing changes and LOE. Charhon stated, "the average of 2026 and 2027 would be fairly similar to the EBITDA that we deliver in 2025."
  • Michael Nedelcovych, TD Cowen, asked about Xifaxan generics and exclusivity through 2028. Appio said, "we know we will have a generic in January 1, 2028... We'll continue to provide updates on these matters as it moves through the court system."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on sustainability of growth, the impact of regulatory changes, and the company's strategy regarding business development and asset monetization.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, using phrases like "exceptional full year results" and "confident the company has a strong team and a diversified portfolio." During Q&A, management was factual and occasionally defensive, reiterating previous guidance and strategies without new commitments, as seen in responses to questions about Bausch + Lomb separation and Xifaxan exclusivity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident, while analysts maintained a focus on future risks and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained its strategic focus on growth and business development, with the addition of the Shibo acquisition in China as a new element.
  • Guidance for 2026 reflects a more moderate growth outlook, with a 3% revenue increase compared to the prior quarter's increased guidance for 2025.
  • Analysts continued to press management on the sustainability of EBITDA amid upcoming regulatory and patent cliffs, a theme consistent with the previous quarter.
  • Management reiterated prior guidance language regarding the impact of regulatory changes, with more explicit discussion of business development flexibility following balance sheet improvements.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and cash flow all showed year-over-year growth, but with management noting certain one-time items that benefited 2025 results.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited nonrecurring revenue drivers benefiting 2025 results, which are not expected to repeat in 2026.
  • The upcoming loss of exclusivity for Xifaxan in January 2028 and Medicare pricing changes in 2027 were identified as material risks to future EBITDA.
  • Solta's revenue was negatively impacted in the quarter by the transition of its Chinese distributor, although management expects improvement moving forward.
  • Increased generic competition in Canada impacted revenues, particularly for Wellbutrin.
  • Analysts questioned the company's ability to offset anticipated revenue losses from patent expirations and regulatory changes, seeking more detail on mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Bausch Health closed 2025 with strong operational and financial results, supported by a consistent execution of its strategic priorities, improvements in capital structure, and targeted business development including the acquisition of Shibo in China. The company projects continued growth for 2026, though at a modest pace, while acknowledging the impact of one-time 2025 benefits and upcoming patent and regulatory headwinds. Management remains focused on leveraging its diversified portfolio, pursuing value-enhancing business development, and maintaining financial flexibility as it navigates future challenges and opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Grand Canyon Education targets high single-digit enrollment growth for 2026 while moderating new site openings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Grand Canyon Education (LOPE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Brian Mueller, Chairman & CEO, reported that "GCE had another strong quarter, producing online enrollment growth of 8.7% and hybrid growth, excluding the closed sites and those in teach-out of 18.7%." He noted GCU now has 25,000 students on campus and more students living in university-owned housing than any other university in the country. Mueller highlighted that GCE and partners have built 47 hybrid campuses nationwide, addressing workforce shortages in healthcare and recently expanded to construction and manufacturing fields. AI integration was emphasized, with dozens of products implemented across academic and operational areas to enhance scale and student outcomes.
  • Mueller outlined that new online starts were up in the mid-single digits, with total enrollment growth of 8.7%, exceeding long-term objectives. He signaled challenging comps in early 2026 but said, "we are rolling out some new programs in the second quarter of this year that we are very excited about that we believe will allow us to continue to grow total enrollment at or slightly above our long-term objectives."
  • Traditional on-campus enrollment was flat year-over-year, with "new traditional campus enrollments... up in the high single digits and total traditional campus enrollments... down slightly year-over-year in the fall of 2025, while total GCU ground enrollment was flat year-over-year." Mueller attributed this to FAFSA issues and higher-than-expected summer graduations but stated that registrations for fall 2026 are "significantly ahead of last year."
  • Hybrid campus enrollment increased 16.6% year-over-year, or 18.7% excluding closed or teach-out sites. Mueller said, "Our goal is still to have 80 locations with our partners with 40 locations being GCU locations," but noted a slowdown in new site openings for 2026 and greater selectivity based on market scalability.
  • Mueller reported service revenue of $308.1 million for Q4 2025, up $15.5 million or 5.3% year-over-year, driven by a 7.1% increase in university partner enrollments. He also highlighted that GCE paid $619 million in taxes over the past seven years while facilitating over 230,000 graduates.
  • Daniel Bachus, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "As adjusted, non-GAAP diluted income per share for the 3 months ended December 31, 2025 and 2024 is $3.21 and $2.95, respectively." Bachus noted higher-than-expected service revenue, operating income of $108.1 million, and an operating margin of 35.1%. He reported $300.1 million in unrestricted cash and investments at year-end and CapEx of $7.6 million for the quarter. Bachus confirmed $284.6 million remains available for share repurchases and the intent to continue aggressive buybacks.

Outlook

  • Bachus explained, "the midpoint of the EPS guidance is above consensus estimates, primarily due to a lower projected share count. The midpoint of the revenue and operating income guidance are generally in line with consensus estimates." For 2026, new online enrollments are projected to be up "in the mid- to high single digits," with total online enrollment growth pressured by graduations and declining reentries. Total enrollment growth is expected in the "mid-single-digit to high single-digit year-over-year growth rate," depending on scenario.
  • Revenue growth for the hybrid pillar will be impacted by contract changes and the teach-out of certain locations, with a $4.2 million reduction in 2026 revenue but an expected positive margin impact. Only one new hybrid site will open in 2026. Bachus said, "We anticipate CapEx for 2026 will be between $30 million and $35 million."
  • GCU ground enrollment is expected to reach "21,900 in the spring," "8,500 to 8,800 in the summer," and "24,900 and 25,600 in the fall," with a "high single-digit increase in new ground enrollments year-over-year" at the midpoint.

Financial Results

  • Service revenue of $308.1 million was reported for Q4 2025, a year-over-year increase of $15.5 million. Operating income reached $108.1 million, with a 35.1% margin. Net income was $86.7 million. GAAP diluted income per share was $3.14, while non-GAAP was $3.21 for the quarter.
  • The fourth quarter effective tax rate was 22.4%. The company repurchased 605,730 shares at a cost of approximately $100 million in Q4 2025, with additional repurchases post-quarter.
  • Bachus reiterated that 2025's performance "significantly exceeded our original estimates, beating the midpoint of the non-GAAP as adjusted diluted net income per share guidance we put out at this time last year by $0.46."

Q&A

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research, questioned the impact of the government shutdown on military tuition assistance. CFO Bachus responded, "It probably was in the $2.5 million to $3 million range."
  • Paris asked about investments in ground campus marketing. Mueller explained, "We experimented in the fall in September and October, spending a significant amount of money and got great results...the conversion rate of those students into registrations is up significantly over where it was at the same time last year."
  • Paris inquired about the high school enrollment counselor headcount. Mueller stated, "We're probably down 10% from a counselor standpoint, where we were the previous year."
  • Paris sought an update on corporate programs, with Mueller noting, "about 1/3 of our online enrollments come from that channel, and that's growing," and a trend toward employer-driven education partnerships.
  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets, asked about regulatory changes. Bachus replied, "our expectation is it's going to have little to no impact" due to GCU's low tuition levels relative to loan caps.
  • Silber also inquired about program pass/fail regulatory metrics. Bachus acknowledged one master's program category failed in preliminary data but said, "Other than that, all the programs at all of our partners passed."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on operational and regulatory impacts, with a neutral tone and some positive feedback, especially regarding marketing investments and enrollment trends.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, repeatedly referencing strong results, effective investments, and positive early indicators for 2026. Mueller stated, "I believe AI will make our current advantages even greater, which makes me even more confident we will continue to meet or exceed our long-term objectives."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst and management sentiment remained steady, with management's tone slightly more assertive about future growth and strategic investments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Online enrollment growth slowed to 8.7% from 9.6% in the prior quarter, while hybrid growth (excluding closed/teach-out sites) decreased to 18.7% from 19.3%.
  • The company continues to shift its marketing and recruitment spend, with accelerated investments in digital marketing for traditional campus recruitment and a reduction in counselor roles.
  • Guidance language remained consistent, with continued emphasis on growth in all three pillars but acknowledgment of tougher comps and more selective expansion in hybrid locations.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on enrollment trends, program diversification, and regulatory impacts, with the latest quarter showing more discussion around marketing strategy effectiveness and corporate partnerships.
  • Management's confidence in AI integration and operational efficiency was more pronounced this quarter, with a greater emphasis on scalability and adaptability as competitive advantages.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted challenging year-over-year comps due to strong prior-year growth, especially in online and hybrid segments.
  • Bachus noted that "the hybrid growth rate is being impacted by the fact that we now have 14 locations that are at or near capacity..."
  • Regulatory changes, including loan caps and accountability calculations, were discussed, but management does not expect material impacts, citing GCU's low tuition and program diversification.
  • The teach-out of certain partner locations and contract modifications will reduce 2026 revenue for the hybrid pillar by $4.2 million, though margins are expected to improve.
  • Increasing graduations and a continued decline in reentries for online programs are pressuring total enrollment growth.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored another quarter of strong growth in online and hybrid enrollment, supported by investments in AI, targeted marketing, and robust employer partnerships. While new site expansion in 2026 will be moderated and some hybrid revenue will decrease due to site closures and contract changes, the company is positioned for margin improvement and continued enrollment growth, with confidence in meeting high single-digit targets for the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: TrueBlue outlines 3% to 9% revenue growth target for Q1 2026 while expanding energy sector footprint
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TrueBlue, Inc. (TBI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Taryn Owen, CEO, stated TrueBlue executed on its strategic priorities in 2025, restructuring the business model to expand sales capability, unlock growth opportunities, and improve profitability while tightly managing costs. Owen reported, "We launched an enterprise-wide strategic partnership with a leading group purchasing organization, unlocking new client acquisition channels and fueling a growing pipeline of multi-brand opportunities across our portfolio. This partnership has led to approximately $15 million of annualized new business wins and continues to build momentum as we expand the relationship into new sectors."

  • Owen noted, "Our energy sector revenue grew 60%, while our commercial driver business continued to outperform the broader market, delivering its second consecutive year of double-digit growth."

  • On the technology front, Owen said, "We've made significant progress enhancing the capabilities of our digital ecosystem with advancements that include embedded AI-powered job matching, predictive analytics and behavioral insights across the talent life cycle. Recently, we launched an AI-enabled bill rate feature within our JobStack app that provides personalized data-driven bill rates in seconds."

  • Carl Schweihs, CFO, stated, "Total revenue for the quarter was $418 million, up 8% and near the high end of our outlook range. Organic revenue increased 5%, with the acquired HSP business contributing 3 percentage points of growth."

  • Schweihs added, "We successfully reduced SG&A by 11%, even while revenue grew 8% for the quarter. This improved leverage demonstrates our continued commitment to managing costs and delivering enhanced profitability."

  • Owen addressed Board changes: "In early 2026, we welcomed 2 highly-qualified independent directors with deep operational and commercial experience and announced that 2 current directors would step down at or before our 2026 Annual Meeting."

Outlook

  • Schweihs stated, "Looking ahead to the first quarter of 2026, we expect revenue growth of 3% to 9% year-over-year as we continue to build on the success we've achieved in recent quarters. This includes 1 percentage point of inorganic growth from HSP."

  • Schweihs provided context on margins: "We expect a lower margin in the first quarter, but our lean cost structure will drive improved margins as we move through the year."

Financial Results

  • Schweihs reported, "Gross margin was 21.5% for the quarter, down from 26.6% in the prior year period, primarily due to less favorability in the prior year workers' compensation reserve adjustments and the changes in revenue mix."

  • The company posted a net loss of $32 million, which included an $18 million noncash long-lived asset impairment charge related to the sublease of the Chicago support office, unlocking over $30 million of cash flow over the remaining 10 years of the lease. Adjusted net loss was $8 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $2 million for the quarter.

  • PeopleReady grew 11%, with energy vertical revenue more than doubling for the second consecutive quarter. PeopleManagement revenue declined 2%. PeopleSolutions revenue grew 42%, driven by the HSP acquisition.

  • The balance sheet showed $25 million in cash, $66 million of debt, and $68 million of borrowing availability, resulting in total liquidity of $92 million at quarter-end.

Q&A

  • Marc Riddick, Sidoti & Company: Asked about margin trajectory and the impact of demand improvements. Schweihs responded, "We've done a really good job managing costs and controlling what we can in this market...with the optimized cost base that we have, we're poised for significant incremental margins and expanding our profitability as demand rebounds."

  • Riddick also inquired about visibility and sustainability of growth in the energy business. Owen stated, "We're very encouraged by the momentum in our energy business, especially in renewables...In quarter 4 alone, we secured several multimillion dollar project wins, and our pipeline remains very healthy."

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: Asked about pass-through costs in renewable energy and gross margin impact. Schweihs answered, "As you kind of think about that significant growth, it resulted in about 200 bps of gross margin contraction, as we've got those pass-through costs that go into that business."

  • Jessica Luce, Northcoast Research: Asked about customer sentiment. Owen said, "I would say overall, our customer sentiment remains cautious due to ongoing uncertainties in the environment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about margin sustainability, energy business visibility, pass-through cost impact, and customer sentiment, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they sought clarity on drivers of growth and profitability.

  • Management maintained an optimistic and confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, emphasizing successful execution, strong sales momentum, and disciplined cost management. Schweihs stated, "we're poised for significant incremental margins and expanding our profitability as demand rebounds."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts demonstrated similar sentiment, with management maintaining confidence and analysts continuing to probe for clarity on margins, client demand, and cost structure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 projects 3% to 9% revenue growth, compared to Q4's actual 8% growth. The margin outlook points to compression due to normalization of workers' compensation reserve adjustments, a shift from the prior quarter's highlight of favorable cost reductions.

  • The energy vertical expanded to 15% of the portfolio at the end of Q4, up from 10% at the end of 2024, with renewables continuing to drive strong growth. Cost discipline and sales model restructuring remained strategic priorities, but Q4 saw the addition of Board refreshment and technology-enabled sales enhancements.

  • Analysts' focus remained on gross margin, cost leverage, and end-market demand, with a continued emphasis on sustainability of recent gains and the impact of pass-through costs.

  • Management's tone remained confident, highlighting operational progress and market share gains, while analysts' tone stayed consistently neutral to cautious.

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin pressure remains a key challenge, particularly due to normalization of workers' compensation reserve adjustments and revenue mix shifts toward lower-margin renewable energy work.

  • Customer sentiment continues to be cautious amid macro uncertainties, affecting visibility for demand recovery.

  • Pricing pressure persists, as Schweihs noted, "We continue to see kind of some pricing pressure in the business. We had -- our pay rates were up about 3.8% in the quarter, while bill rates were up 2.5%."

  • The company is not currently prioritizing new M&A, instead focusing on managing the business to cash flow positive and evaluating opportunities as they arise.

Final Takeaway

TrueBlue's Q4 2025 call emphasized continued organic revenue growth, strong momentum in the energy sector, disciplined cost management, and strategic Board refreshment. Management projects 3% to 9% revenue growth for Q1 2026, with expectations for margin normalization as workers' compensation tailwinds subside. While customer sentiment remains cautious and margin pressures persist, the company remains focused on expanding its market share in high-growth sectors and leveraging technology and operational efficiencies to drive sustainable, profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil futures surge by most in four months on rising U.S.-Iran fears, Russia-Ukraine deadlock
data.description:
Crude oil futures rallied for their biggest daily gain since October on Wednesday, as traders weighed potential supply disruptions from any conflict between the U.S. and Iran, with a report warning that American military intervention in Iran could come sooner than expected.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery closed +4.6% to $65.19/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for April delivery ended +4.3% to $70.35/bbl; both benchmarks posted their biggest one-day gains since October 23 and highest settlements since January 30, rebounding a day after sinking to two-week lows.

Both contracts rallied near the end of the session following reports that Israel had raised its alert level due to increased indications of a possible attack on Iran by the U.S. and Israel, according to Price Futures Group analyst Phil Flynn.

Axios reported earlier that a U.S. military campaign against Iran likely would be "a massive, weeks-long campaign that would look more like a full-fledged war," and the Trump administration is "closer to a major war in the Middle East than most Americans realize."

A war could put flows at risk from a region that pumps about a third of the world's oil, but President Trump would risk angering voters ahead of November's midterm elections if a spike in crude prices makes gasoline more expensive at the pump.

"Iran has leverage over the oil price via the Strait of Hormuz," SEB chief commodities analyst Bjarne Schieldrop said in a note. "That is a pain-point directed towards U.S. consumers who will deliver their votes in the U.S. midterm elections later this year."

Meanwhile, peace talks between Russia and Ukraine in Geneva ended after only two hours, with Ukraine's President Zelenskiy accusing Russia of stalling.

"There has been a renewed effort to clamp down on Russian exports, so if these talks do go off the rails as Zelenskiy suggested they are, then we could finally see a material drop in the amount of Russian exports headed to the global market, and that is obviously supportive," Again Capital's John Kilduff said in a note.

U.S. natural gas futures edged lower as a warmer weather outlook reduced demand expectations for the tail end of winter; front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for March delivery ended -0.7% to $3.011/MMBtu, its lowest settlement value since October 17.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Jack in the Box reiterates 2026 guidance as debt reduction and value-driven promotions take center stage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Jack in the Box Inc. (JACK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lance Tucker highlighted a strategic shift, stating, "We remain focused on simplifying the business, and we've made visible progress since the last quarter." He noted the completion of the Del Taco sale in December and a significant paydown on debt, adding, "We are doing exactly what we committed to do by simplifying the business and bringing down debt levels, and I'm really pleased with the progress to date." The CEO emphasized the launch of the 75th anniversary marketing calendar, which drove higher sales of Munchie Meals and a positive customer response to new promotions. Tucker also pointed to operational improvements, saying, "Last quarter, we identified a gap in field support and restructured that team... meaning we have a greatly increased presence in the restaurants to give more real-time support to our franchisees and team members."
  • Tucker explained the company’s ongoing focus: "We are beginning to see early results that reinforce that we are on the right path. But as a reminder, this is a multistep process, and the benefits of this work will take time to fully materialize."
  • CFO Dawn Hooper reported, "The first quarter same-store sales for Jack in the Box decreased 6.7%, comprised of franchise restaurant same-store sales decrease of 7% and a company-owned same-store sales decrease of 4.7%." Hooper added, "Jack's restaurant level margin percentage in the quarter decreased to 16.1%, down from 23.2%. Food and packaging costs as a percentage of sales were 29.7% for the quarter, increasing 380 basis points from the prior year."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed previous guidance, with Tucker stating, "as the reaffirmation of our guidance reflects, we expect to see steady improvement on the top line as we move through 2026."
  • Hooper confirmed, "we are reiterating our guidance from November 2025."
  • Hooper maintained expectations for commodity inflation: "our guidance still stands. We had guided to mid-single digits back in November. Beef is definitely the most impactful. Q1 actually came in a little higher than we had anticipated, but you can look to see beef up double digits, and as the year continues, that impact will moderate."

Financial Results

  • Hooper detailed, "Franchise-level margin was $84.1 million or 38.6% of franchise revenues compared to $97.1 million or 40.9% a year ago."
  • The company reported six restaurant openings and fourteen closures in the quarter.
  • Hooper said, "SG&A for the quarter was $37 million or 10.6% of revenues compared to $41.2 million or 11.1% a year ago."
  • Hooper added, "Earnings from continuing operations was $14.4 million for the first quarter of 2026 as compared to $31 million for the first quarter of the prior year," with GAAP diluted earnings per share from continuing operations at $0.75 compared to $1.61 a year ago.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $68.2 million, down from $88.8 million in the prior year.
  • The company made a partial prepayment of $105 million on its August 2026 debt tranche, with total debt outstanding at quarter end at $1.6 billion and a net debt to adjusted EBITDA leverage ratio of 6.5x.

Q&A

  • Alexander Slagle, Jefferies LLC: Asked about January trends and weather impacts. Tucker responded, "we're really seeing kind of same-store sales play out the way we thought they would. We started the second quarter really a couple of hundred basis points better than we were in the first quarter, and that's before the weather impact... So when you factor out the weather, we're really low single digits right now."
  • Pratik Patel, Barclays: Inquired about franchisee four-wall margins and support. Tucker explained, "we are seeing pressure on four-wall EBITDA right now between the sales conditions and then beef inflation in particular. So at this point, no, we're not doing any kind of blanket assistance, but we are looking at what we need to do in those kind of one-off cases where we have a franchisee struggling."
  • Andrew Charles, TD Cowen: Questioned commodity inflation. Hooper replied, "Beef is definitely the most impactful. Q1 actually came in a little higher than we had anticipated, but you can look to see beef up double digits, and as the year continues, that impact will moderate."
  • Karen Holthouse, Citi: Asked about the remodel program. Tucker stated, "this is really more cosmetic... paint... re-striping and sealing the parking lot. It's cleaning up the landscaping... And that can be done for a very, very low cost. I'm talking under $20,000, and franchisees tend to have a way of getting things done more cheaply than we do."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin pressures, inflation, and operational challenges, with a slightly negative tone in questions about franchisee health, commodity inflation, and competitive pressures.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone in prepared remarks, but responses in the Q&A were more defensive and focused on operational details and mitigation strategies. Tucker frequently reiterated ongoing efforts and the multistep nature of performance improvement.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains cautious, but management showed more specificity in discussing cost control, operational restructuring, and marketing tactics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was reaffirmed, with no material change in the company’s outlook language from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from closing the Del Taco transaction to operational improvements and executing value-driven marketing for the Jack in the Box brand.
  • Management is now emphasizing field support restructuring and simplified marketing, compared to broader discussions of brand transformation and restructuring in the prior quarter.
  • Key metrics, including same-store sales decline and margin compression, show continued operational headwinds. Analysts continue to probe on franchisee margins and inflation, consistent with the previous quarter’s concerns.
  • Management’s tone has shifted from outlining a transition to reporting initial progress and tangible results, though caution persists.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing challenges with commodity inflation, especially in beef costs, and elevated labor expenses, particularly in the Chicago market.
  • Hooper noted, "we expected Chicago to have elevated labor in the quarter, and while the market did improve throughout the quarter, there is still work to be done."
  • Franchisee profitability remains under pressure, with Tucker stating, "the four-wall margins are not where they need to be, but we're doubling down, doing a lot on the profitability side."
  • Cost pressures from utilities and other operating expenses also contributed to margin declines.
  • Analysts raised concerns about regional performance, competitive value positioning, and the pace of operational recovery in challenging markets.

Final Takeaway

Jack in the Box management reiterated its commitment to debt reduction, operational simplification, and targeted marketing as part of the JACK on Track and Jack’s Way plans. While first quarter results reflected persistent sales and margin headwinds, early 2026 marketing initiatives and operational changes are beginning to show positive momentum, particularly in sales trends post-January and localized restaurant refresh programs. Management remains focused on executing its multi-step turnaround, emphasizing a disciplined approach to cost control, franchisee support, and customer value, with the expectation that these actions will drive steady improvement through the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
